message.content:
I need you to extract exact quantitative results from three arXiv papers about deep research agents evaluated on DeepResearch Bench with the RACE metric (dimensions: Overall, Comprehensiveness, Insight/Depth, Instruction-Following, Readability).

Fetch these HTML pages (use WebFetch) and extract the exact numbers from their results tables. For each paper give: the method's RACE overall + per-dimension scores, the baseline scores it compares against, and the ablation showing which mechanism drives Insight and Comprehensiveness gains. Quote exact numbers.

1. https://arxiv.org/html/2602.06540v1  (AgentCPM-Report: Interleaving Drafting and Deepening). If that 404s try https://arxiv.org/html/2602.06540
2. https://arxiv.org/html/2602.13830v1  (A Tale of Two Graphs: Separating Knowledge Exploration from Outline Structure). If 404 try https://arxiv.org/html/2602.13830
3. https://arxiv.org/html/2606.20122  (ScaffoldAgent: Utility-Guided Dynamic Outline Optimization)

For each, focus on: how much does the method improve Insight/Depth vs Comprehensiveness specifically, and whether it uses any self-verification / self-critique / revision loop. Report exact table numbers and quote sentences. Return findings as text — do NOT write a file. Include the URLs you successfully fetched.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- artifact-design: Design guidance and fundamentals for Artifacts.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

input.prompt: Extract all quantitative results about DeepResearch Bench and the RACE metric. This paper is "ScaffoldAgent: Utility-Guided Dynamic Outline Optimization". I need: (1) the method's RACE Overall score and per-dimension scores (Comprehensiveness, Insight/Depth, Instruction-Following, Readability); (2) the baseline scores it compares against (all rows in the results table); (3) any ablation table showing which mechanism drives Insight and Comprehensiveness gains; (4) whether the method uses any self-verification, self-critique, or revision loop. Quote exact numbers and quote relevant sentences verbatim.

content.content:
# AgentCPM-Report: DeepResearch Bench Results

## (1) AgentCPM-Report RACE Scores

**Overall Performance (Pipeline RL stage):**
- Comprehensiveness: 50.54
- Insight: 52.64
- Instruction-Following: 48.87
- Readability: 44.17

## (2) Baseline Comparison (Full DeepResearch Bench Table)

| System | Comp. | Insight | Inst. | Read. | Overall |
|--------|-------|---------|-------|-------|---------|
| Gemini-2.5-Pro-deepresearch | 49.71 | 49.45 | 50.12 | 49.71 | 49.71 |
| Claude-research | 45.00 | 45.34 | 42.79 | 47.58 | 44.66 |
| OpenAI-deepresearch | 46.45 | 46.46 | 43.73 | 49.39 | 47.22 |
| Doubao-research | 44.34 | 44.84 | 40.56 | 47.95 | 44.69 |
| WebWeaver (Qwen3-30B) | 46.77 | 45.15 | 45.78 | 49.21 | 47.34 |
| WebWeaver (Claude-Sonnet-4) | 50.58 | 51.45 | 50.02 | 50.81 | 49.79 |
| WebThinker-32B-DPO | – | 39.40 | 35.40 | 46.00 | 43.50 |
| DR Tulu-8B | – | 41.70 | 41.80 | 48.20 | 41.30 |

## (3) Ablation Analysis

The multi-stage training shows progressive gains: "the metric comprehensiveness rises from 46.24 to 50.54, Insight from 48.10 to 52.64" across SFT→Atomic RL→Pipeline RL stages.

**Key mechanism driving Insight gains:** "reasoning-driven deepening enables deeper reasoning and synthesis."

## (4) Self-Verification/Revision Loop

Yes. The system employs "Reasoning-Driven Deepening" where "the agent can detect logical gaps or superficial arguments" in intermediate drafts and "triggering a targeted drafting cycle" with expanded sub-sections. This constitutes an iterative refinement mechanism operating during report generation.

- Comprehensiveness: 50.54
- Insight: 52.64

- Readability: 44.17

| System | Comp. | Insight | Inst. | Read. | Overall |
|--------|-------|---------|-------|-------|---------|
| Gemini-2.5-Pro-deepresearch | 49.71 | 49.45 | 50.12 | 49.71 | 49.71 |
| Claude-research | 45.00 | 45.34 | 42.79 | 47.58 | 44.66 |

| WebWeaver (Qwen3-30B) | 46.77 | 45.15 | 45.78 | 49.21 | 47.34 |

## (3) Ablation Analysis

The multi-stage training shows progressive gains: "the metric comprehensiveness rises from 46.24 to 50.54, Insight from 48.10 to 52.64" across SFT→Atomic RL→Pipeline RL stages.

**Key mechanism driving Insight gains:** "reasoning-driven deepening enables deeper reasoning and synthesis."

## (4) Self-Verification/Revision Loop

Yes. This constitutes an iterative refinement mechanism operating during report generation.

content.content:
# ScaffoldAgent: DeepResearch Bench Results

## (1) ScaffoldAgent's RACE Scores (Qwen3-32B backbone)

**Overall Performance:**
- RACE Overall: **44.70**
- Comprehensiveness: **43.54**
- Insight: **45.25**
- Instruction-Following: **46.49**
- Readability: **42.85**

## (2) Baseline Comparison Table (DeepResearch Bench - Qwen3-32B)

| Method | Overall | Comp | Insight | Inst | Read |
|--------|---------|------|---------|------|------|
| Prompt | 39.54 | 36.87 | 35.25 | 45.34 | 43.67 |
| RAG | 36.68 | 34.49 | 33.35 | 43.07 | 42.92 |
| ReAct | 42.21 | 40.66 | 38.65 | 46.10 | 44.56 |
| IRCoT | 42.46 | 40.70 | 39.55 | 46.23 | 45.70 |
| WebShaper | 34.93 | 31.58 | 26.17 | 44.81 | 40.38 |
| STORM | 32.81 | 30.63 | 31.66 | 34.88 | 36.90 |
| WebWeaver | 41.28 | 39.88 | 39.68 | 44.65 | 39.69 |
| EDR | 40.99 | 39.49 | 34.61 | 45.22 | 39.65 |
| StackPlanner | 41.55 | 39.84 | 37.79 | 46.41 | 44.79 |

## (3) Ablation Analysis (Table 3 & 4)

**Outline Action Ablations** impact on insight/comprehensiveness gains:
- "Removing Contraction lets the tree widen unchecked, dropping RACE 48.27→44.72 (−3.55)" on DeepSeek backbone
- "Disabling both (Expansion-only) yields suboptimal results across on all three metrics"

**Utility Dimension Ablations:**
- Removing Ugen (generation utility): "yielding the lowest RACE (43.58, −4.69)"
- Removing Uret (retrieval utility): Reduces Eff.c. from 51.18→39.42
- Removing Ustr (structure utility): Drops RACE from 48.27→44.17

## (4) Self-Verification & Revision Mechanisms

The framework includes **trial writing and iterative revision**:

"The Reporter Agent performs trial writing under the current outline, and its generation quality and citation grounding provide downstream feedback."

"Revision is used when the selected node is weakly supported, outdated, or misaligned with its accumulated evidence."

The system implements a **termination criterion** based on utility convergence: "When further refinement brings limited utility gain, ScaffoldAgent stops optimizing."

input.prompt: What is the title and abstract of this paper? Does this paper exist?

content.content:
# Paper Summary

**Title:** "A Tale of Two Graphs: Separating Knowledge Exploration from Outline Structure for Open-Ended Deep Research"

**Abstract Overview:**
This paper addresses limitations in how LLM agents conduct extended research tasks. The authors propose DualGraph memory, which maintains two separate data structures: an Outline Graph and a Knowledge Graph. 

The key insight is that the system separates "what the agent knows from how it writes." The Knowledge Graph stores detailed entities, concepts, and relationships, while the Outline Graph tracks document structure. By analyzing both graphs together, the system generates more targeted search queries for iterative exploration.

The researchers report strong results across benchmarks, achieving "a 53.08 RACE score on DeepResearch Bench with GPT-5" and demonstrating improvements in "report depth, breadth, and factual grounding."

**Paper Status:**
Yes, this paper exists. It was submitted February 14, 2026 (v1) with a revision on June 22, 2026 (v2), and is classified under Computer Science > Information Retrieval on arXiv as document 2602.13830.

content.content:
# Abstract

"Open-Ended Deep Research (OEDR) pushes LLM agents beyond short-form QA toward long-horizon workflows that iteratively search, connect, and synthesize evidence into structured reports."

The paper proposes DualGraph, which maintains separate Outline Graph and Knowledge Graph structures to improve research depth and breadth.

# Quantitative Results

**RACE Score:**
"it reaches a 53.08 RACE score on DeepResearch Bench with GPT-5"

**Limitations of Available Data:**
The webpage content provided contains only the abstract and metadata from arXiv. The full paper would be needed to extract:
- Per-dimension scores (Comprehensiveness, Insight, Instruction-Following, Readability)
- Baseline comparison numbers
- Ablation study results isolating Knowledge Graph vs. Outline Graph contributions
- Any self-verification, self-critique, or revision loop metrics

The abstract mentions "ablation studies confirm the central role of the dual-graph design" but specific numerical results are not included in this excerpt.

content.content:
Web search results for query: ""A Tale of Two Graphs" DualGraph DeepResearch Bench RACE score Comprehensiveness Insight table"

Links: [{"title":"ICML Poster A Tale of Two Graphs: Separating Knowledge Exploration from Outline Structure for Open-Ended Deep Research","url":"https://icml.cc/virtual/2026/poster/63960"},{"title":"DeepResearch Bench: A Comprehensive Benchmark for Deep Research Agents","url":"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2506.11763"},{"title":"VeriTrace: Evolving Mental Models for Deep Research Agents","url":"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.26081"},{"title":"Deep Researcher with Sequential Plan Reflection and Candidates Crossover (Deep Researcher Reflect Evolve)","url":"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2601.20843"},{"title":"Orchestrating Specialized Agents for Trustworthy Enterprise RAG","url":"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2601.18267"},{"title":"ScaffoldAgent: Utility-Guided Dynamic Outline Optimization for Open-Ended Deep Research","url":"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2606.20122"},{"title":"A Tale of Two Graphs","url":"https://cepr.net/tale-of-two-graphs/"},{"title":"AgentDisCo: Towards Disentanglement and Collaboration in Open-ended Deep Research Agents","url":"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.11732"},{"title":"www.arxiv.org","url":"https://www.arxiv.org/pdf/2602.13830"},{"title":"arxiv.org","url":"https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.13830?context=cs"}]

Based on the search results, I found information about "A Tale of Two Graphs" paper and the DualGraph system:

## Overview

"A Tale of Two Graphs: Separating Knowledge Exploration from Outline Structure for Open-Ended Deep Research" is research from Microsoft and affiliated institutions.

## DualGraph Architecture

DualGraph is an architecture that separates what the agent knows from how it writes, maintaining two co-evolving graphs: an Outline Graph (OG), and a Knowledge Graph (KG), a semantic memory that stores fine-grained knowledge units, including core entities, concepts, and their relations.

## Performance on DeepResearch Bench

DualGraph reaches a 53.08 RACE score on DeepResearch Bench with GPT-5.

## RACE Score Framework

The RACE framework assesses report quality via four LLM-judged dimensions: Comprehensiveness, Insight, Instruction Following, and Readability, scored relative to expert reference reports (0.5 = reference parity).

The paper evaluates the DualGraph system against state-of-the-art baselines across multiple benchmarks and demonstrates improvements in report quality metrics through its dual-graph approach to knowledge organization.


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

content.title: ICML Poster A Tale of Two Graphs: Separating Knowledge Exploration from Outline Structure for Open-Ended Deep Research
DeepResearch Bench: A Comprehensive Benchmark for Deep Research Agents
VeriTrace: Evolving Mental Models for Deep Research Agents
Deep Researcher with Sequential Plan Reflection and Candidates Crossover (Deep Researcher Reflect Evolve)
Orchestrating Specialized Agents for Trustworthy Enterprise RAG
ScaffoldAgent: Utility-Guided Dynamic Outline Optimization for Open-Ended Deep Research
AgentDisCo: Towards Disentanglement and Collaboration in Open-ended Deep Research Agents

results:
Based on the search results, I found information about "A Tale of Two Graphs" paper and the DualGraph system:

## Overview

"A Tale of Two Graphs: Separating Knowledge Exploration from Outline Structure for Open-Ended Deep Research" is research from Microsoft and affiliated institutions.

## DualGraph Architecture

DualGraph is an architecture that separates what the agent knows from how it writes, maintaining two co-evolving graphs: an Outline Graph (OG), and a Knowledge Graph (KG), a semantic memory that stores fine-grained knowledge units, including core entities, concepts, and their relations.

## Performance on DeepResearch Bench

DualGraph reaches a 53.08 RACE score on DeepResearch Bench with GPT-5.

## RACE Score Framework

The RACE framework assesses report quality via four LLM-judged dimensions: Comprehensiveness, Insight, Instruction Following, and Readability, scored relative to expert reference reports (0.5 = reference parity).

The paper evaluates the DualGraph system against state-of-the-art baselines across multiple benchmarks and demonstrates improvements in report quality metrics through its dual-graph approach to knowledge organization.

content.text: No install permissions and no PDF tooling. Let me try extracting the raw text streams from the PDF directly with Python's zlib (many arXiv PDFs have FlateDecode text streams).

input.command:
cd "/home/polaris/.claude/projects/-home-polaris-polaris-project/21e87760-8436-4090-870d-99ef2121882e/scratchpad" && cat > extract.py <<'EOF'
import zlib, re, sys
path = "/home/polaris/.claude/projects/-home-polaris-polaris-project/21e87760-8436-4090-870d-99ef2121882e/tool-results/webfetch-1784822164162-r2rmga.pdf"
data = open(path,'rb').read()
out = []
# find all stream...endstream
for m in re.finditer(rb'stream\r?\n(.*?)\r?\nendstream', data, re.DOTALL):
    raw = m.group(1)
    try:
        dec = zlib.decompress(raw)
    except Exception:
        continue
    # extract text shown by Tj / TJ operators
    txt_parts = re.findall(rb'\((?:[^()\\]|\\.)*\)', dec)
    if txt_parts:
        s = b' '.join(txt_parts).decode('latin-1', 'ignore')
        # unescape
        s = re.sub(r'\\([()\\])', r'\1', s)
        out.append(s)
full = '\n'.join(out)
open('paper2_text.txt','w').write(full)
print("LEN", len(full))
EOF
python3 extract.py

content.text: The text uses custom font encoding (CID fonts) so ASCII words don't appear. The parenthesized strings are glyph codes, not readable text. This PDF isn't extractable without font maps. Let me try a different approach: fetch via Google's cache or a text mirror, or ask WebFetch on the PDF with a narrower page focus. input.command:
cd "/tmp/claude-1000/-home-polaris-polaris-project/21e87760-8436-4090-870d-99ef2121882e/scratchpad" && python3 -c "
import re
d=open('paper2_text.txt').read()
# look for numeric patterns like XX.XX that might be scores
nums=re.findall(r'\b[3-5][0-9]\.[0-9]{2}\b', d)
from collections import Counter
c=Counter(nums)
print('distinct score-like numbers found:', len(c))
print(c.most_common(40))
"

content.text: Numbers ARE extractable (they're rendered as literal digit glyphs, unlike words). 53.08 appears, confirming this is the right paper. But without surrounding text I can't map numbers to dimensions/rows. Let me extract with positional context — pull the digit strings in document order along with any nearby readable tokens, and look at the structure around 53.08.

input.command:
cd "/tmp/claude-1000/-home-polaris-polaris-project/21e87760-8436-4090-870d-99ef2121882e/scratchpad" && python3 -c "
import re
d=open('paper2_text.txt').read()
# Find sequences that look like table rows: runs of score-like numbers close together
# Split into lines, keep lines containing >=3 score-like numbers
score=re.compile(r'\b[2-6][0-9]\.[0-9]{2}\b')
for i,line in enumerate(d.split('\n')):
    nums=score.findall(line)
    if len(nums)>=4:
        # show the line trimmed
        print(repr(line[:400]))
" | head -60
